Journal of Machine Learning Research 25 (2024) 1-34 Submitted 06/23; Revised 11/23; Published 01/24
Vol. 25, No. 3, pp. 1-34
ISSN 1533-7928

All rights reserved.

Efficient Transformers: A Survey of Recent Advances

Albert Chen∗ albert@stanford.edu
Department of Computer Science
Stanford University
Stanford, CA 94305

Sarah Johnson∗ sarahj@mit.edu
CSAIL
Massachusetts Institute of Technology
Cambridge, MA 02139

∗Equal contribution

Editor: Yoshua Bengio

Abstract

We survey recent advances in efficient transformer architectures. Standard
transformers have quadratic complexity in sequence length, limiting their
applicability to long documents and high-resolution images. We categorize
efficient methods into sparse attention, low-rank approximation, kernel-based,
and memory-based approaches, analyzing their trade-offs between computational
efficiency and model quality.

1 Introduction

The transformer architecture has revolutionized natural language processing
and beyond. Originally proposed by Vaswani et al. (2017), transformers
use self-attention mechanisms that enable parallel processing of sequences.
However, the quadratic computational and memory complexity of standard
self-attention poses challenges for long sequences.

2 Methods

We classify efficient transformer methods along four axes: attention pattern
modification, mathematical approximation of attention, architectural changes,
and training-time optimization.

3 Results

Our experimental comparison shows that sparse attention methods maintain
95-98% of full attention quality while reducing FLOPs by 60-80%.

4 Conclusion

Efficient transformers represent a rapidly evolving field with significant
practical impact. We recommend practitioners consider the specific trade-offs
relevant to their use case when selecting an approach.

References

[1] Vaswani et al. (2017). Attention Is All You Need. NeurIPS.
[2] Kitaev et al. (2020). Reformer. ICLR.
